title: Learning Gaussian optical states with quantum computers
authors: Spencer Dimitroff, John Kallaugher, Ashe Miller, Mohan Sarovar
abstract: Recent results have established dramatic advantages in learning properties of quantum states when a quantum computer is available to process or jointly measure multiple copies of the unknown quantum state. Learning tasks can be accomplished with exponentially fewer copies of the state when compared to optimized classical learning strategies that are restricted to measuring one copy of the state at a time. While these results were established in abstract settings and for artificial learning tasks, they motivate the application of quantum computers to imaging and sensing of weak electromagnetic fields since these settings are ultimately concerned with the learning of unknown quantum states. In this work we apply these new results in quantum learning to the problem of learning Gaussian states of the electromagnetic field, which are germane since they describe most fields used in imaging and sensing. In order to connect with quantum learning theory, we consider the transduction of an $n$-mode Gaussian state into a register of qubits on a quantum computer followed by optimized measurements on these qubits to extract the parameters defining the original Gaussian state. We rigorously bound the number of copies of the Gaussian state required to achieve worst-case additive error in parameter estimates. The scaling of this bound with $n$ is exponentially better than naïve strategies for characterizing Gaussian states and matches recently derived bounds for characterization of Gaussian states using continuous-variable (CV) classical shadows. In addition, our bound has a polynomially better dependence on the energy of the multimode Gaussian state compared to the CV shadows protocol.
summary: This paper proposes a method to use quantum computers to efficiently learn the properties of multi-mode Gaussian optical states. It demonstrates that by mapping optical information onto qubits, one can achieve a sample complexity that scales logarithmically with the number of modes and more efficiently with energy than classical methods.
main_problem: Determining the sample complexity required to learn all parameters (means and covariances) of an n-mode Gaussian optical state using a quantum computer.
main_result: The authors establish that the number of required state copies scales logarithmically with the number of modes n, providing a polynomial advantage in energy dependence compared to existing CV classical shadow protocols.
method: The protocol uses a Jaynes-Cummings interaction to transduce optical modes into a qubit register, followed by an iterative algorithm and classical shadows to extract Gaussian moments from Pauli measurements.
model_or_system: An n-mode continuous-variable bosonic electromagnetic field interacting with an n-qubit register via a local Jaynes-Cummings Hamiltonian.
key_observables: One- and two-qubit Pauli observables (e.g., X, Y, Z, XX, YY) on the transduced qubit register.
important_parameters: Number of modes (n), maximum energy per mode (E_max), target additive error (epsilon), and failure probability (delta).
main_assumptions: The optical state is Gaussian; the Jaynes-Cummimng interaction is kept in the perturbative regime; and the transduction process is ideal (ignoring experimental noise).
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the transduction schematic; Figure 2 illustrates the inductive proof for state selection; Figure 3 demonstrates the rapid convergence of the error reduction algorithm.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of Gaussian state learning, defines the QCIS transduction model via Jaynes-Cummings interaction, provides a mathematical reduction of the n-mode problem to a two-mode problem, presents an iterative algorithm for parameter extraction, and concludes with a complexity analysis comparing the results to CV classical shadows.
why_interesting: It bridges quantum information theory with quantum optics by providing a rigorous scaling analysis for using quantum hardware to perform high-precision sensing and imaging of electromagnetic fields.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
